content:
# Ephesians Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/EPH_002_page_001.jpg, EPH_002_page_002.jpg

1. And you were dead in the trespasses and sins

2. in which you once walked, following the course of this world, following the prince of the power of the air, the spirit that is now at work in the sons of disobedience —

3. among whom we all once lived in the passions of our flesh, carrying out the desires of the body and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath, like the rest of mankind.

4. But God, being rich in mercy, because of the great love with which he loved us,

5. even when we were dead in our trespasses, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you have been saved —

6. and raised us up with him and seated us with him in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus,

7. so that in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus.

8. For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God,

9. not a result of works, so that no one may boast.

10. For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them.

11. Therefore remember that at one time you Gentiles in the flesh, called "the uncircumcision" by what is called "the circumcision," which is made in the flesh by hands —

12. remember that you were at that time separated from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world.

13. But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ.

14. For he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility

15. by abolishing the law of commandments expressed in ordinances, that he might create in himself one new man in place of the two, so making peace,

16. and might reconcile us both to God in one body through the cross, thereby killing the hostility.

17. And he came and preached peace to you who were far off and peace to those who were near.

18. For through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father.

19. So then you are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God,

20. built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone,

21. in whom the whole structure, being joined together, grows into a holy temple in the Lord.

22. In him you also are being built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 2 divides into two major sections: salvation by grace (vv.1-10) and the union of Jew and Gentile (vv.11-22). All three translators captured both the theological and social dimensions of the argument.

**Verses 4-5 — "But God":** All three translators preserved the dramatic contrast. SOPHIA affirms: the syntactic interruption (three verses of human deadness answered by "but God") is the rhetorical center of the passage.

**Verses 14-16 — "One new man":** All three translators recognized the creation of a new humanity. SOPHIA affirms: Paul's vision is not assimilation but creation — something that did not exist before.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "being dead" vs. "you were dead" vs. "you were dead because of your disobedience"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And you being dead in your trespasses and sins" | hymas ontas nekrous — accusative participle |
| LOGOS | "And you were dead in the trespasses and sins" | Finite verb — smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "Once you were dead because of your disobedience and your many sins" | "Because of" — causal; adds "many" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek is a participle (ontas, "being") not a finite verb, but English requires a finite verb here. PNEUMA adds "because of" (changing the dative to causal) and "many" (not in Greek).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds causal relationship and "many."

---

### v.2 — "the ruler of the authority of the air" vs. "the prince of the power of the air" vs. "the devil — the commander of the powers in the unseen world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the ruler of the authority of the air" | ton archonta tēs exousias tou aeros — triple genitive |
| LOGOS | "the prince of the power of the air" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the devil — the commander of the powers in the unseen world" | Identifies as "the devil"; "commander"; "unseen world" for "air" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says "the ruler of the authority of the air" — a deliberately mysterious description. PNEUMA identifies this figure as "the devil" (Paul doesn't name him here), changes "ruler" to "commander," and changes "the air" to "the unseen world" — an interpretive substitution.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA identifies, militarizes, and reinterprets.

---

### v.2 — "the sons of the disobedience" vs. "the sons of disobedience" vs. "those who refuse to obey God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the sons of the disobedience" | tois huiois tēs apeitheias — Hebraism |
| LOGOS | "the sons of disobedience" | Same — preserves Hebraism |
| PNEUMA | "those who refuse to obey God" | Dynamic rendering; adds "God" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Sons of disobedience" is a Hebraism meaning "those characterized by disobedience." LOGOS preserves the idiom. PNEUMA renders the meaning but adds "God" — Paul says simply "disobedience," not specifying its object.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "God" and loses Hebraism.

---

### v.3 — "children of wrath" vs. "subject to God's anger"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we were by nature children of wrath" | tekna physei orgēs — Hebraism |
| LOGOS | "were by nature children of wrath" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "By our very nature we were subject to God's anger" | "Subject to God's anger"; adds "God's" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Children of wrath" is a Hebraism meaning "those deserving of/subject to wrath." PNEUMA renders the meaning but adds "God's" — Paul says "wrath" without specifying whose.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds divine attribution.

---

### v.5 — "made us alive together with the Christos" vs. "he gave us life when he raised Christ from the dead"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "made us alive together with the Christos" | synezōopoiēsen tō Christō — "co-made-alive with Christ" |
| LOGOS | "made us alive together with Christ" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he gave us life when he raised Christ from the dead" | Adds "when he raised Christ from the dead" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. synzōopoieō means "make alive together with." PNEUMA adds "when he raised Christ from the dead" — connecting co-vivification to resurrection, which Paul does not explicitly do until v.6.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA anticipates v.6.

---

### v.8 — "this not of yourselves — it is the gift of God" — touto reference

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and this not of yourselves — [it is] the gift of God" | touto (neuter) — "this [thing]" |
| LOGOS | "And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God" | Same — "this" = whole salvation event |
| PNEUMA | "And you can't take credit for this; it is a gift from God" | "Can't take credit" — colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. touto (neuter) cannot grammatically refer to pistis (faith, feminine) or charis (grace, feminine) — it refers to the entire salvation arrangement. LOGOS and EMET preserve this. PNEUMA's "can't take credit" captures the sense but colloquializes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — register.

---

### v.10 — "his workmanship" vs. "God's masterpiece"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we are his workmanship" | poiēma — "thing made/work of art" |
| LOGOS | "we are his workmanship" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "we are God's masterpiece" | "Masterpiece" — elevates |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. poiēma means "thing made/created work" (English "poem" derives from the same root). "Workmanship" is the standard rendering. PNEUMA's "masterpiece" adds a qualitative judgment not in the Greek — poiēma simply means "something made."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA elevates "workmanship" to "masterpiece."

---

### v.11 — "the nations in the flesh" vs. "you Gentiles in the flesh" vs. "you Gentiles used to be outsiders"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you, the nations in [the] flesh, the [ones] called 'uncircumcision'" | ta ethnē en sarki |
| LOGOS | "you Gentiles in the flesh, called 'the uncircumcision'" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "you Gentiles used to be outsiders. You were called 'uncircumcised heathens' by the Jews" | "Outsiders"; "uncircumcised heathens"; identifies the callers as "the Jews" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul uses the technical terms "uncircumcision" and "circumcision" as labels. PNEUMA changes "uncircumcision" to "uncircumcised heathens" (adding "heathens" — a pejorative not in the Greek) and identifies the callers as "the Jews" (Paul says "what is called the circumcision").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "heathens" and identifies.

---

### v.14 — "the middle wall of the partition, the hostility" vs. "the dividing wall of hostility" vs. "the wall of hostility that separated us"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the middle wall of the partition, the hostility, in his flesh" | to mesotoichon tou phragmou, tēn echthran, en tē sarki autou |
| LOGOS | "has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility" | Consolidated; "in his flesh" repositioned |
| PNEUMA | "in his own body on the cross, he broke down the wall of hostility that separated us" | "Body on the cross" for "flesh"; adds "that separated us" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek syntax is complex — "the middle wall of the fence, the hostility" with "in his flesh" modifying the action. LOGOS consolidates cleanly. PNEUMA adds "on the cross" (not until v.16 in the Greek) and "that separated us."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA anticipates cross reference and adds.

---

### v.15 — "the law of the commandments in ordinances" vs. "the law of commandments expressed in ordinances" vs. "the system of law with its commandments and regulations"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the law of the commandments in ordinances" | ton nomon tōn entolōn en dogmasin |
| LOGOS | "the law of commandments expressed in ordinances" | "Expressed in" — clarifies relationship |
| PNEUMA | "the system of law with its commandments and regulations" | "System of law" — interpretive; "regulations" for dogmata |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The compressed phrase describes the specific legal regulations that created the Jew-Gentile boundary. LOGOS renders the genitive chain faithfully. PNEUMA's "system of law" is an interpretive paraphrase.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA paraphrases compressed phrase.

---

### v.15 — "one new man" vs. "one new man" vs. "one new people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "one new man" | hena kainon anthrōpon — "one new human being" |
| LOGOS | "one new man" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "one new people from the two groups" | "People" for anthrōpon; adds "from the two groups" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. anthrōpos is singular — "one new man/human being." Paul uses the singular deliberately: from two peoples, one person. PNEUMA changes the singular to plural "people" and adds "from the two groups."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "One new man" (singular anthropos) vs. "one new people" (collective). Paul's singular is a christological statement — the new humanity is one person, echoing the "one body" and "one new man" language. The singular is theologically richer than the plural.

---

### v.19 — "strangers and sojourners" vs. "strangers and aliens" vs. "strangers and foreigners"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "strangers and sojourners" | xenoi kai paroikoi |
| LOGOS | "strangers and aliens" | Same terms |
| PNEUMA | "strangers and foreigners" | "Foreigners" for paroikoi |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Minor vocabulary difference — all three capture the resident-alien status. paroikos means "one who dwells beside/near" — a non-citizen resident.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Trivial variant.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 6 | "The devil," "many," "God's anger," "on the cross," "heathens," "from the two groups" |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 3 | Identifies unnamed figures (v.2), anticipates later verses (vv.5, 14), changes singular to plural (v.15) |
| Hebraism preservation | 2 | "Sons of disobedience" (v.2), "children of wrath" (v.3) |
| "One new man" singular | 1 | Singular anthropos vs. collective "people" — christological (v.15) |
| Cosmic power description | 1 | "Prince of the power of the air" vs. "the devil" (v.2) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 2's most significant disagreement is the rendering of "one new man" (v.15) — whether Paul envisions a singular new humanity (one person, Christ, in whom Jew and Gentile are united) or a collective new people. The singular is Paul's deliberate christological point: the new entity is one person, not just one group. PNEUMA also continues the pattern of identifying unnamed figures ("the devil" in v.2) and adding divine attribution ("God's anger" in v.3) where Paul leaves these ambiguous.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
